retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The results of the SURPASS trials demonstrate that tirzepatide yields clinically significant improvements in glycemic control and weight loss when compared with other GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide and dulaglutide), insulin degludec, and insulin glargine. Consequently, the American Diabetes Association (ADA) categorizes tirzepatide as a highly effective therapy for achieving glycemic control and weight loss. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide/insulin-degludec has obtained FDA approval, affirming their effectiveness and safety in diabetes management. [12] [13] Because of safety concerns, research on another agent, taspoglutide, has been halted in phase III trials. [14] Recently, Orforglipron, a nonpeptide oral GLP-1 receptor agonist, has demonstrated promising results in phase 2 clinical trials. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Indications -- FDA-approved GLP-1 receptor agonists for glycemic control include:
retrieved_docs.content: Dulaglutide (subcutaneous-SC) Exenatide injectable solution subcutaneous Exenatide injectable suspension SC Liraglutide SC Liraglutide/insulin degludec Lixisenatide/insulin glargine Semaglutide (oral, SC) Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist)

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Current clinical data demonstrated that tirzepatide is superior to placebo in improving hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) levels. The SURPASS-5 clinical trial showed a -2.11% reduction in HbA1c levels at 5 mg per week dosing, compared to -0.86% with a placebo. At the highest dose of 15 mg per week, tirzepatide led to a -2.34% reduction in HbA1c. This was demonstrated over 40 weeks. A weight reduction of 5.4 kg was seen with 5 mg of tirzepatide dosing, and a 10.5 kg reduction was observed with 15 mg dosing. This dose-dependent correlation with weight loss is similar to semaglutide—a common GLP-1 medication utilized for weight loss management. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA approved the first GLP-1RA exenatide in 2005. The oral semaglutide tablet was approved by the FDA in 2019. Except for oral semaglutide, all the other drugs are subcutaneous injections. Albiglutide was discontinued in 2017 due to a decrease in sales. [2]  Similarly, lixisenatide has been recently withdrawn from the US market, a decision driven by commercial considerations rather than safety or efficacy concerns. According to ADA, the combination of basal insulin and GLP-1RA demonstrates potent glucose-lowering effects while minimizing weight gain and reducing the risk of hypoglycemia compared to intensified insulin regimens. Currently, 2 distinct once-daily, pre-mixed dual-combination products are available, which include lixisenatide with insulin glargine and liraglutide with insulin degludec. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: As per the 2023 ADA guidelines, GLP-1 receptor agonists are recommended for mitigating cardiovascular risk. These agents not only lower the chances of cardiovascular events and hypoglycemia but also demonstrate potential in potentially decreasing the progression of chronic kidney disease (CKD). GLP-1 agonists are recommended for individuals with a history of clinical ASCVD (eg, prior myocardial infarction, stroke. [9] [10] GLP-1 agonists, with proven cardiovascular benefits, include Liraglutide, subcutaneous Semaglutide, and Dulaglutide. [11] Structurally, these agents fall into 2 broad categories: human GLP-1 backbone agents and exendin-4 backbone agents. Below are FDA-approved GLP-1 agonists. Human GLP-1 backbone: Dulaglutide Albiglutide - discontinued Liraglutide Semaglutide Exendin-4 backbone: Exenatide (2 formulations) Lixisenatide - discontinued Tirzepatide is a GIP analog that activates both the GLP-1 and GIP receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tirzepatide is a novel medication approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in May 2022 for treating type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). Tirzepatide is a synthetic polypeptide and dual agonist for the glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptors. Therefore, the drug leads to significantly improved glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with T2DM, maximizing benefits similar to GLP-1 medications such as semaglutide. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Semaglutide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as 3 separate brand name medications—Ozempic ® , Wegovy ® , and Rybelsus ® . Each brand name comes with its own associated indications, preparations, and dosages to consider. The Rybelsus ® tablet brand demonstrates efficacy in improving glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). Ozempic ® injectable brand provides an additional indication of reducing the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with and without T2DM. In a recent development, semaglutide received FDA approval under the brand name Wegovy ® , which is an injection used for promoting weight loss in individuals dealing with obesity and overweight. Due to the increased complexity involved, special attention is necessary for every FDA-approved indication and current off-label use.

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tirzepatide is currently utilized as a second-line diabetes medication akin to GLP-1 drugs, such as semaglutide, and is administered once weekly via subcutaneous (SQ) injection with incremental dosage adjustments. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Semaglutide -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA has approved using semaglutide for weight loss, marketed under the brand name Wegovy ® . The recommended standard maintenance dosage for weight management is 2.4 mg, administered through weekly subcutaneous self-injections. With growing interest in semaglutide for weight control, healthcare professionals are responding to the demand by occasionally prescribing alternative brands of semaglutide, such as Ozempic ® and Rybelsus ® , for off-label use (using a drug approved by the FDA for a different purpose). Differences in insurance coverage between Wegovy® versus Ozempic® and Rybelsus® may also fuel this off-label use.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Clinical Significance -- Semaglutide
retrieved_docs.content: Marketed under Ozempic, Rybelsus, and Wegovy, semaglutide is the latest long-acting glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1RA) approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for obesity treatment. It has shown significant weight loss benefits in patients with and without diabetes, along with improvements in glycemic control. While oral and injectable forms are FDA-approved for diabetes treatment and have demonstrated weight loss effects, only the subcutaneous preparation has received FDA approval specifically for obesity treatment. [15] Several studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of semaglutide in promoting weight loss among individuals who are overweight or obese, irrespective of whether they have type 2 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Semaglutide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as 3 separate brand name medications—Ozempic®, Wegovy ® , and Rybelsus ® . Each brand name comes with its own associated indications, preparations, and dosages to consider. Due to the increased complexity involved, special attention is necessary for every FDA-approved indication and current off-label use.

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: GLP-1 agonists (also known as GLP-1 receptor agonists, incretin mimetics, or GLP-1 analogs) represent a class of medications used to treat T2DM and, in some cases, obesity. Examples of drugs in this class include Exenatide, Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, and Semaglutide. According to the American Diabetes Association (ADA), metformin remains the preferred first-line therapy for treating T2DM. However, the addition of a GLP-1 analog should be considered in patients with a contraindication or intolerance to metformin, in patients with a hemoglobin A1c greater than 1.5% over target, or in patients who do not reach their target A1c in 3 months, particularly in patients with atherosclerosis, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease. [1] [2] [3] [4] Furthermore, Semaglutide and high-dose Liraglutide are FDA-approved as pharmacologic treatments for obesity or can be prescribed to overweight patients with comorbidities. The utilization of GLP-1 analogs is an area of research with favorable hemoglobin A1c results and weight loss results in patients with type-1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM). Of note, higher costs, as well as tolerability, remain significant barriers to prescribing these medications. [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Semaglutide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: T2DM and reduction of major cardiovascular events: The SUSTAIN clinical trials have exhibited favorable outcomes in reducing hemoglobin A1C (HbA1c)  in patients with T2DM through the weekly subcutaneous semaglutide brand name Ozempic ® . These trials compared semaglutide injection to placebo (SUSTAIN 1), sitagliptin (SUSTAIN 2), exenatide extended-release (SUSTAIN 3), daily insulin glargine with metformin and/or a sulfonylurea (SUSTAIN 4), and basal insulin with or without metformin (SUSTAIN 5). [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] Each trial demonstrated statistically significant reductions in HbA1c with semaglutide.

retrieved_docs.title: History Taking in Obesity Medicine: A Comprehensive Guide -- Function -- Food and Drug Administration-approved obesity medications assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Note: Tirzepatide (Zepbound): Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist; FDA-approved in 2023 for chronic weight management in adults with a BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity. [20] Tirzepatide was initially approved for type 2 diabetes as Mounjaro in May 2022.

retrieved_docs.title: Alogliptin -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved alogliptin in January 2013 to improve glycemic control in adults with T2D. As a dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor in the "gliptin" class, alogliptin is used alongside diet and exercise to enhance glycemic control. [1] [2] [3] The approval in 2013 included 3 formulations—a standalone agent, a combination with metformin, and a combination with pioglitazone. A multicentered, double-blind, randomized trial showed that initiating triple therapy with metformin, alogliptin, and pioglitazone provides greater efficacy and long-term stability compared to dual add-on therapy in patients with T2D who are insufficiently controlled on metformin monotherapy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine / lixisenatide is a fixed‑dose combination medication that contains the active component lixisenatide, a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) receptor agonist, together with insulin glargine; this relationship clarifies that lixisenatide is a constituent of the insulin glargine / lixisenatide formulation, which is important for understanding its pharmacologic composition and therapeutic role in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise agents such as liraglutide and lixisenatide. Liraglutide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, while lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine / lixisenatide. Both combination products, together with insulin glargine alone, are classified within the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This illustrates how GLP‑1 receptor agonists are paired with basal insulin therapies to provide long‑acting injectable regimens that enhance glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues for injection, long-acting is a therapeutic category that includes insulin glargine as a standalone long‑acting insulin analogue and the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs the basal insulin with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist lixisenatide. Both agents are classified as long‑acting injectable insulins and are used to provide basal glucose control in patients with diabetes, with the combination also offering post‑prandial glucose reduction through lixisenatide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine and the combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide are both classified as members of the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This category comprises long‑acting injectable insulin analogues that are used to provide basal insulin coverage in the management of diabetes, helping to maintain stable blood glucose levels over extended periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacotherapy of diabetes is organized into two principal subclasses: insulins and analogues (injectable agents) and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, long‑acting formulations are classified as “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting,” a subset of the broader insulins and analogues class. Long‑acting insulin products include insulin glargine (also recorded as “inzulin glargin”), the fixed‑ratio combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the fixed‑ratio combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide; the latter two pair a basal insulin with the GLP‑1 analogues liraglutide and lixisenatide respectively, providing complementary glycaemic control. Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are grouped under “blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and comprise several mechanistic families: alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors (which delay carbohydrate absorption), DPP‑4 inhibitors such as alogliptin and sitagliptin (the latter

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue provides both basal insulin coverage and incretin‑mediated glucose control in a single injection. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized: insulin‑based therapies are distinguished from non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, and within each group, drugs are further classified by mechanism, duration of action, and formulation, facilitating precise selection and combination of therapies for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 0 -- Community 17
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues, together with non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, form the primary pharmacologic categories for diabetes management, while antilipemic agents and fibrates address dyslipidemia. Within the insulin family, long‑acting formulations (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec) and intermediate‑ or long‑acting preparations combined with fast‑acting agents (e.g., human insulin aspart, “Insulin, Beef”) constitute the “Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” subclass; insulin aspart is a rapid‑acting analogue that binds the human insulin‑receptor (INSR) and physiologically decreases lipolysis. GLP‑1 analogues—exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide—are non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs; liraglutide and lixisenatide are incorporated into fixed‑dose combinations with long‑acting insulins (insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide), which themselves are classified as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: long‑acting agents are used to provide basal insulin coverage. GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS EXCLUDING INSULINS. Members of the GLP‑1 analogue class include EXENATID, LIRAGLUTIDE, and LIXISENATIDE. LIRAGLUTIDE is a component of the combination product INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE, while LIXISENATIDE is contained within the combination product INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE, illustrating how GLP‑1 agents are incorporated into mixed insulin/GLP‑1 therapies. ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS, BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING, are also members of the BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS EXCLUDING INSULINS category, providing an additional oral mechanism to reduce post‑prandial glucose excursions. BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS form another subclass of BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS EXCLUDING INSULINS. This group includes METFORMIN (the prototypical biguanide) and its misspelled variant FENFORMIN, both of which act by decreasing hepatic glucose

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide is classified as a member of the glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue class, and the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec/liraglutide includes liraglutide as an integral component of its formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues constitute a major subclass of drugs used in diabetes, encompassing both traditional insulin preparations and newer peptide‑based agents. Within this group, formulations for injection are further divided into categories based on pharmacokinetic profiles: intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins combined with fast‑acting agents, and long‑acting insulins administered alone. Long‑acting insulin products include insulin glargine, insulin degludec, and combination preparations such as insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide. These combination products pair a basal insulin component with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue, thereby providing complementary glucose‑lowering mechanisms. GLP‑1 analogues themselves form a distinct subclass of peptide therapeutics and include exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. Liraglutide is a constituent part of the insulin degludec / liraglutide combination, while lixisenatide is incorporated into the insulin glargine / lixisenatide formulation. Animal‑derived insulin preparations, such as insulin derived from beef, are also classified within the category of intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins combined with rapid‑acting agents, reflecting their role as protein‑based therapeutic agents. Overall, the taxonomy highlights that insulins and analogues—ranging from pure basal insulin formulations to hybrid insulin‑GLP‑1 products—are integral components of diabetes pharmacotherapy, with each subclass and specific agent contributing to tailored glycemic control strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that includes the subclass insulins and analogues. Within insulins and analogues, the group Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting is defined as a specific type of long‑acting injectable insulin therapy. This long‑acting group comprises individual members such as insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. The combination product insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 receptor agonist lixisenatide together with the basal insulin insulin glargine. Together, these hierarchical and compositional relationships clarify how basal insulin agents and basal insulin/GLP‑1 combination products are organized within diabetes pharmacotherapy, informing clinicians’ choices of long‑acting insulin regimens for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “drugs used in diabetes” comprises insulin‑based products (insulins and analogues) and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Within the insulin family, long‑acting injectable analogues such as insulin glargine, insulin degludec and combination formulations that pair a basal insulin with a rapid‑acting component (e.g., beef insulin and human insulin) are classified as long‑acting or intermediate/long‑acting plus fast‑acting insulins. Fixed‑dose combinations that couple a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—provide complementary glycaemic control. The GLP‑1 analogue class includes exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin, sitagliptin) and biguanides (e.g., fenformin/metformin). Sitagliptin is incorporated in a fixed‑dose tablet with the statin simvastatin; simvastatin is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antiretroviral nelfinavir mesylate. This taxonomy links basal long‑acting insulin analogues, oral biguanides, DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues, highlighting their distinct mechanisms, common combination products, and important drug‑interaction considerations in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES comprise two major families: INSULINS AND ANALOGUES and BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. INSULINS AND ANALOGUES include both intermediate‑ or long‑acting formulations combined with fast‑acting agents (e.g., the category “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting”) and long‑acting injectable preparations (e.g., “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting”). Specific products that belong to these categories are INSULIN DEGLUDEC / LIRAGLUTIDE, INSULIN GLARGINE / LIXISENATIDE, INZULIN GLARGIN, INZULIN LIDSKÝ, and INSULIN, BEEF. INSULIN, BEEF is also classified as a protein and is a member of the intermediate‑/long‑acting combined‑with‑fast‑acting group. INZULIN LIDSKÝ is a member of the same combined group, while INZULIN GLARGIN, INSULIN DEGLUDEC / LIRAGLUTIDE and INSULIN GLARGINE / LIXISENATIDE are members of the long‑acting injection group. INSULIN DEGLUDEC / LIRAGLUTIDE
